{ z } from \\\"zod\\\";\\nimport { BookingStatus, BookingType } from \\\"@prisma/client\\\";\\nimport { db } from \\\"@/lib/db\\\";\\nimport { getCurrentUser } from \\\"@/lib/currentUser\\\";\\nimport { startOfTodayLocal } from \\\"@/lib/dates\\\";\\n\\nexport const runtime = \\\"nodejs\\\";\\n\\nconst ParamsSchema = z.object({\\n  type: z.nativeEnum(BookingType),\\n});\\n\\nexport async function GET(_req: Request, ctx: { params: Promise<{ type: string }> }) {\\n  const user = await getCurrentUser();\\n  if (!user) return NextResponse.json({ error: \\\"Unauthenticated\\\" }, { status: 401 });\\n\\n  const paramsRaw = await ctx.params;\\n  const parsedParams = ParamsSchema.safeParse({ type: paramsRaw.type });\\n  if (!parsedParams.success) return NextResponse.json({ error: \\\"Invalid queue type\\\" }, { status: 400 });\\n\\n  const type = parsedParams.data.type;\\n  const serviceDate = startOfTodayLocal();\\n\\n  const dailyMessage = await db.dailyMessage.findUnique({\\n    where: { serviceDate },\\n    select: { message: true, launchStartTime: true, retrievalStartTime: true, updatedAt: true },\\n  });\\n\\n  if (user.role === \\\"ADMIN\\\") {\\n    const pending = await db.booking.findMany({\\n      where: { type, serviceDate, status: BookingStatus.PENDING },\\n      orderBy: [{ requestedAt: \\\"asc\\\" }, { id: \\\"asc\\\" }],\\n      select: {\\n        id: true,\\n        requestedAt: true,\\n        completedAt: true,\\n        stayingOnMooring: true,\\n        vessel: {\\n          select: {\\n            id: true,\\n            yardNumber: true,\\n            boatName: true,\\n            boatType: true,\\n            boatNumber: true,\\n            owner: { select: { id: true, name: true, username: true, email: true, phone: true } },\\n          },\\n        },\\n      },\\n    });\\n\\n    const completed = await db.booking.findMany({\\n      where: { type, serviceDate, status: BookingStatus.COMPLETED },\\n      orderBy: [{ completedAt: \\\"desc\\\" }, { requestedAt: \\\"asc\\\" }],\\n      take: 200,\\n      select: {\\n        id: true,\\n        requestedAt: true,\\n        completedAt: true,\\n        stayingOnMooring: true,\\n        vessel: {\\n          select: {\\n            id: true,\\n            yardNumber: true,\\n            boatName: true,\\n            boatType: true,\\n            boatNumber: true,\\n            owner: { select: { id: true, name: true, username: true, email: true, phone: true } },\\n          },\\n        },\\n      },\\n    });\\n\\n    return NextResponse.json({\\n      serviceDate: serviceDate.toISOString(),\\n      dailyMessage: dailyMessage ?? null,\\n      pending,\\n      completed,\\n      counts: { pending: pending.length, completed: completed.length },\\n    });\",\"numLines\":80,\"startLine\":1,\"totalLines\":118}}"</outcome>

\\\"next/server\\\";\\n\\ninterface TideEvent {\\n  time: string;\\n  height: number;\\n  type: \\\"high\\\" | \\\"low\\\";\\n}\\n\\nexport async function GET(req: NextRequest) {\\n  const { searchParams } = new URL(req.url);\\n  const lat = searchParams.get(\\\"lat\\\");\\n  const lon = searchParams.get(\\\"lon\\\");\\n  const timezone = searchParams.get(\\\"timezone\\\") || \\\"America/Vancouver\\\";\\n\\n  if (!lat || !lon) {\\n    return NextResponse.json({ error: \\\"lat and lon required\\\" }, { status: 400 });\\n  }\\n\\n  try {\\n    const fetchPromises: Promise<Response>[] = [\\n      fetch(\\n        `https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast?latitude=${lat}&longitude=${lon}&current=temperature_2m,wind_speed_10m,wind_direction_10m,weather_code,is_day&daily=sunrise,sunset&wind_speed_unit=kmh&timezone=${encodeURIComponent(timezone)}`,\\n        { next: { revalidate: 300 } }\\n      ),\\n      fetch(\\n        `https://marine-api.open-meteo.com/v1/marine?latitude=${lat}&longitude=${lon}&current=wave_height,wave_direction,wave_period&timezone=${encodeURIComponent(timezone)}`,\\n        { next: { revalidate: 300 } }\\n      ),\\n    ];\\n\\n    // WorldTides API for accurate high/low tide predictions\\n    // Sign up free at https://www.worldtides.info (100 lookups/day)\\n    // Add WORLDTIDES_API_KEY to your .env.local or Vercel env vars to enable tides\\n    const tidesKey = process.env.WORLDTIDES_API_KEY;\\n    if (tidesKey) {\\n      const today = new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10);\\n      fetchPromises.push(\\n        fetch(\\n          `https://www.worldtides.info/api/v3?extremes&lat=${lat}&lon=${lon}&key=${tidesKey}&days=2&date=${today}`,\\n          { next: { revalidate: 3600 } }\\n        )\\n      );\\n    }\\n\\n    const results = await Promise.all(fetchPromises);\\n    const weather = await results[0].json();\\n    const marine = await results[1].json();\\n\\n    let tides: TideEvent[] | null = null;\\n    if (tidesKey && results[2]) {\\n      const tidesData = await results[2].json();\\n      const now = new Date();\\n      const extremes = (tidesData.extremes ?? []) as Array<{ dt: number; height: number; type: string }>;\\n      tides = extremes\\n        .filter((e) => new Date(e.dt * 1000) > now)\\n        .slice(0, 4)\\n        .map((e) => ({\\n          time: new Date(e.dt * 1000).toISOString(),\\n          height: Math.round(e.height * 10) / 10,\\n          type: e.type === \\\"High\\\" ? \\\"high\\\" : \\\"low\\\",\\n        }));\\n    }\\n\\n    return NextResponse.json({\\n      temperature: weather.current?.temperature_2m ?? null,\\n      windSpeed: weather.current?.wind_speed_10m ?? null,\\n      windDirection: weather.current?.wind_direction_10m ?? null,\\n      weatherCode: weather.current?.weather_code ?? null,\\n      isDay: weather.current?.is_day ?? null,\\n      sunrise: weather.daily?.sunrise?.[0] ?? null,\\n      sunset: weather.daily?.sunset?.[0] ?? null,\\n      waveHeight: marine.current?.wave_height ?? null,\\n      wavePeriod: marine.current?.wave_period ?? null,\\n      tides,\\n    });\\n  } catch {\\n    return NextResponse.json({ error: \\\"Failed to fetch conditions\\\" }, { status: 500 });\\n  }\\n}\\n\",\"numLines\":80,\"startLine\":1,\"totalLines\":80}}"</outcome>
</observed_from_primary_session>

content.thinking:
They've added two new functions to dashboard-client.tsx:

1. `startEditVessel(v: VesselRow)` - Initiates edit mode for a vessel by:
   - Setting the editing vessel ID
   - Populating the edit form with the vessel's current data
   
2. `saveEditVessel(e: React.FormEvent, vesselId: string)` - Saves vessel edits by:
   - Making a PATCH request to /api/vessels/[id]
   - Handling errors
   - Closing edit mode and refreshing the page on success

This is the backend logic for editing. Now they need to add the UI components (edit form/buttons in the vessel cards). Let me continue observing.

content.thinking:
Perfect! They've now added the inline edit form that appears when a vessel is being edited. The form includes:
- 4 input fields (boat name, registration #, boat type, yard #)
- Save and Cancel buttons
- Loading state during save
- Compact styling to fit within the vessel card

This completes the vessel editing feature (Requirement 6 part 1). The feature now has:
1. PATCH API endpoint ✓
2. State management ✓
3. Edit button ✓
4. Inline edit form ✓
5. Save/cancel functionality ✓

This is a complete, working feature for customers to edit their vessel details. 

Now I have observed a substantial amount of work:
- Requirement 3: Queue refresh intervals changed from 3 min/60 sec to 10 seconds (3 files modified)
- Requirement 6: Vessel editing implemented (2 files modified - API + UI)

This is a good point to create an observation summarizing the work done. Let me wait to see if there's any more immediate work or if this is a stopping point.

content.text: *Observing: Added inline edit form UI for vessels - completes full vessel editing feature (Requirement 6 backend + frontend implementation)*

Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>switch to cloud</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-03-26</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

Your task is to create a detailed summary of the conversation so far, paying close attention to the user's explicit requests and your previous actions.
This summary should be thorough in capturing technical details, code patterns, and architectural decisions that would be essential for continuing development work without losing context.

Before providing your final summary, wrap your analysis in <analysis> tags to organize your thoughts and ensure you've covered all necessary points. In your analysis process:

1. Chronologically analyze each message and section of the conversation. For each section thoroughly identify:
   - The user's explicit requests and intents
   - Your approach to addressing the user's requests
   - Key decisions, technical concepts and code patterns
   - Specific details like:
     - file names
     - full code snippets
     - function signatures
     - file edits
   - Errors that you ran into and how you fixed them
   - Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
2. Double-check for technical accuracy and completeness, addressing each required element thoroughly.

Your summary should include the following sections:

1. Primary Request and Intent: Capture all of the user's explicit requests and intents in detail
2. Key Technical Concepts: List all important technical concepts, technologies, and frameworks discussed.
3. Files and Code Sections: Enumerate specific files and code sections examined, modified, or created. Pay special attention to the most recent messages and include full code snippets where applicable and include a summary of why this file read or edit is important.
4. Errors and fixes: List all errors that you ran into, and how you fixed them. Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
5. Problem Solving: Document problems solved and any ongoing troubleshooting efforts.
6. All user messages: List ALL user messages that are not tool results. These are critical for understanding the users' feedback and changing intent.
7. Pending Tasks: Outline any pending tasks that you have explicitly been asked to work on.
8. Current Work: Describe in detail precisely what was being worked on immediately before this summary request, paying special attention to the most recent messages from both user and assistant. Include file names and code snippets where applicable.
9. Optional Next Step: List the next step that you will take that is related to the most recent work you were doing. IMPORTANT: ensure that this step is DIRECTLY in line with the user's most recent explicit requests, and the task you were working on immediately before this summary request. If your last task was concluded, then only list next steps if they are explicitly in line with the users request. Do not start on tangential requests or really old requests that were already completed without confirming with the user first.
                       If there is a next step, include direct quotes from the most recent conversation showing exactly what task you were working on and where you left off. This should be verbatim to ensure there's no drift in task interpretation.

Here's an example of how your output should be structured:

<example>
<analysis>
[Your thought process, ensuring all points are covered thoroughly and accurately]
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   [Detailed description]

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - [Concept 1]
   - [Concept 2]
   - [...]

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - [File Name 1]
      - [Summary of why this file is important]
      - [Summary of the changes made to this file, if any]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [File Name 2]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [...]

4. Errors and fixes:
    - [Detailed description of error 1]:
      - [How you fixed the error]
      - [User feedback on the error if any]
    - [...]

5. Problem Solving:
   [Description of solved problems and ongoing troubleshooting]

6. All user messages: 
    - [Detailed non tool use user message]
    - [...]

7. Pending Tasks:
   - [Task 1]
   - [Task 2]
   - [...]

8. Current Work:
   [Precise description of current work]

9. Optional Next Step:
   [Optional Next step to take]

</summary>
</example>

Please provide your summary based on the conversation so far, following this structure and ensuring precision and thoroughness in your response. 

There may be additional summarization instructions provided in the included context. If so, remember to follow these instructions when creating the above summary. Examples of instructions include:
<example>
## Compact Instructions
When summarizing the conversation focus on typescript code changes and also remember the mistakes you made and how you fixed them.
</example>

<example>
# Summary instructions
When you are using compact - please focus on test output and code changes. Include file reads verbatim.
</example>


IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any tools. You MUST respond with ONLY the <summary>...</summary> block as your text output.

The primary Claude Code session worked on the westcoast-marine-parks project, implementing several requirements from a feature list. Let me analyze chronologically: **Phase 1: Discovery and Requirements Analysis (extensive exploration)** - Performed grep searches to find refresh intervals, location patterns, booking completion logic - Read multiple queue client files, API routes, dashboard components - Invoked an Explore subagent that analyzed 8 requirements and produced a detailed status report - The exploration identified what was complete vs. what needed implementation **Phase 2: Implementation Work (actual code changes)** Requirement 3 - Queue Refresh Intervals: - Modified src/app/queue/combined-queue-client.tsx: - Line 65: Changed `3 * 60 * 1000` to `10 * 1000` - Line 268: Updated UI text from "auto-refreshes every 3 minutes" to "auto-refreshes every 10 seconds" - Modified src/app/office/admin-office-client.tsx: - Line 134: Changed `60 * 1000` to `10 * 1000` with comment update - Modified src/app/queue/queue-client.tsx: - Lines 45, 244: Updated UI text to "auto-refreshes every 10 seconds" - Line 471: Changed interval from `3 * 60 * 1000` to `10 * 1000` Requirement 6 - Vessel Editing: - Created PATCH endpoint in src/app/api/vessels/[id]/route.ts (47 new lines): - Authentication and authorization checks - Validates and updates boatName, boatNumber, boatType, yardNumber - Boat name validation (cannot be empty) - Error handling for Prisma errors - Modified src/app/dashboard/dashboard-client.tsx: - Added state: editingVesselId, editVesselForm, editVesselPending - Added startEditVessel() function to initiate edit mode - Added saveEditVessel() function to PATCH vessel data - Added "Edit" button next to "Remove" button in vessel cards - Added inline edit form with 4 fields (boat name, registration #, boat type, yard #) - Form appears when vessel is being edited, replaces booking buttons area Requirement 7 - Pwllheli Weather (started but not complete): - Modified src/components/conditions-widget.tsx: - Added PWLLHELI constant with hardcoded coordinates (52.9263, -4.4138, Europe/London timezone) - Simplified ConditionsWidget to single panel showing only Pwllheli - Removed geolocation logic, destination search, forecast selectors - Moved old multi-location widget code to _UnusedDestWidget() function - Widget now shows "Destination Conditions" title with Pwllheli data only **Final user message:** "switch to cloud" - appears to be unrelated to the development work, possibly a configuration change request. **Key observations:** - 3 requirements were worked on (3, 6, 7) - Requirements 3 and 6 appear complete - Requirement 7 is partially complete (widget simplified but may need layout adjustments) - 5 files were modified total - No errors encountered during implementation 1. Primary Request and Intent: The primary session was implementing features for the westcoast-marine-parks project based on 8 documented requirements. After an extensive exploration phase using an Explore subagent, the session implemented: - Requirement 3: Change queue refresh intervals from 3 minutes/60 seconds to 10 seconds across all queue views - Requirement 6: Enable customers to edit their vessel details (boat name, registration #, boat type, yard #) - Requirement 7: Simplify weather widget to show only Pwllheli location (hardcoded) instead of geolocation-based display 2. Key Technical Concepts: - Next.js 15 App Router architecture with server and client components - React client components with "use client" directive - setInterval-based auto-refresh patterns in queue views - RESTful API routes with PATCH method for updates - Prisma ORM for database operations - Authentication and authorization patterns (getCurrentUser) - Inline editing UI patterns with state management - Geolocation vs. hardcoded location data for weather widgets - Timezone handling (Europe/London for Pwllheli) 3. Files and Code Sections: - **src/app/queue/combined-queue-client.tsx** - Customer/public view of combined launch and retrieval queues - Changed refresh interval from 3 minutes to 10 seconds: ```typescript // Line 65 - changed from: 3 * 60 * 1000 const refreshInterval = setInterval(() => { if (active) load(); }, 10 * 1000); ``` - Updated UI text from "auto-refreshes every 3 minutes" to "auto-refreshes every 10 seconds" (line 268) - **src/app/office/admin-office-client.tsx** - Admin office view for managing queues - Changed refresh interval from 60 seconds to 10 seconds: ```typescript // Line 134 - changed from: 60 * 1000 const interval = setInterval(() => { if (active) load(true);
